coredata.dc:title: Novel spectrofluorometric method utilizing functionalized carbon quantum dots for determining histamine levels in nasal secretions: Implications for allergic rhinitis
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Alaqel, Saleh l.
Algarni, Majed A.
Alharbi, Adnan
Almalki, Atiah H.
Alzahrani, Mohammad S.
Abdelazim, Mohamed H.
Abdelazim, Ahmed H.
coredata.dc:description: Histamine plays a crucial role in regulating various physiological functions and is significantly involved in the development of allergic rhinitis. Accurately measuring histamine levels in nasal secretions can serve as a specific indicator for studying mast cell activation and diagnosing allergic reactions. However, the controversy surrounding histamine levels in nasal secretions during allergenic stimulation suggests potential inaccuracies and imprecision in the analytical methods used, as well as the presence of substances that may interfere with the determination of histamine. In this study, a sensitive and selective spectrofluorometric method was developed using carbon quantum dots (CQDs) derived from beetroot slices, which are non-toxic and eco-friendly materials. These CQDs were functionalized with histamine tetra phenyl borate to determine histamine levels in nasal secretions. Various characterization techniques were employed to confirm the successful synthesis and functionalization of the CQDs. The functionalized CQDs demonstrated enhanced fluorescence and selective interaction with histamine, leading to concentration-dependent fluorescence quenching. The developed method was successfully utilized to compare histamine levels in nasal secretions of healthy individuals and patients with allergic rhinitis. The results showed that the histamine level was significantly higher in patients with allergic rhinitis than in healthy individuals, suggesting a potential association between allergic rhinitis and histamine, (normal vs. patient: 350 ± 20 ng/mL vs. 2520 ± 37 ng/mL, p < 0.05). Furthermore, the proposed spectrofluorometric method exhibited improved linearity range, dynamic range, and detection limit compared to a previously reported spectrophotometric method.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Spectrofluorometric method
Carbon quantum dots
Beetroot
Histamine
Allergic rhinitis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of specific histamine tetra phenyl borate
Preparation of CQDs from beetroot slices
Functionalization of the prepared beetroots- CQDs with histamine tetra phenyl borate
Assessment of histamine levels in nasal secretions: Comparative evaluation of healthy volunteers and patients with allergic rhinitis
Synthesis and characterization of functionalized CQDs
Optical properties of the proposed functionalized CQDs
Proposed interaction and quenching of the functionalized CQDs by histamine; quantitative analysis impact
Spectrofluorometric determination of histamine in the nasal secretions: Comparison between healthy volunteers and patients with allergic rhinitis
Comparative evaluation of the proposed method and previously reported spectrophotometric for histamine determination

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Deanship of Scientific Research, King Saud University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The researchers would like to acknowledge Deanship of Scientific Research, Taif University for funding this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns of non-functionalized and functionalized CQDs, (b) TEM image showing the diameter distribution of non-functionalized CQDs, and (c) TEM image showing the diameter distribution of functionalized CQDs.
FTIR spectra of non-functionalized and functionalized CQDs.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum, (b) fluorescence spectrum, and (c) excitation-dependent emission spectrum of the functionalized CQDs.
Fluorescence spectra obtained from the quenching reaction of functionalized CQDs with different concentrations of histamine.

Optimization of experimental parameters influencing the stability of fluorescence quenching of the functionalized CQDs by histamine, including (a) buffer pH, (b) buffer volume, (c) CQDs volume, and (d) incubation time.
Method performance and validation data obtained with the proposed method.
Application of the proposed method for the determination of histamine in spiked nasal secretions.
Assessment of histamine in the human nasal secretions of normal subjects and patients with allergic rhinitis.
Comparison of proposed spectrofluorometric method and previously reported spectrophotometric method for histamine determination.

head.ce:title: Novel spectrofluorometric method utilizing functionalized carbon quantum dots for determining histamine levels in nasal secretions: Implications for allergic rhinitis

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Histamine plays a crucial role in regulating various physiological functions and is significantly involved in the development of allergic rhinitis. Accurately measuring histamine levels in nasal secretions can serve as a specific indicator for studying mast cell activation and diagnosing allergic reactions. However, the controversy surrounding histamine levels in nasal secretions during allergenic stimulation suggests potential inaccuracies and imprecision in the analytical methods used, as well as the presence of substances that may interfere with the determination of histamine. In this study, a sensitive and selective spectrofluorometric method was developed using carbon quantum dots (CQDs) derived from beetroot slices, which are non-toxic and eco-friendly materials. These CQDs were functionalized with histamine tetra phenyl borate to determine histamine levels in nasal secretions. Various characterization techniques were employed to confirm the successful synthesis and functionalization of the CQDs. The functionalized CQDs demonstrated enhanced fluorescence and selective interaction with histamine, leading to concentration-dependent fluorescence quenching. The developed method was successfully utilized to compare histamine levels in nasal secretions of healthy individuals and patients with allergic rhinitis. The results showed that the histamine level was significantly higher in patients with allergic rhinitis than in healthy individuals, suggesting a potential association between allergic rhinitis and histamine, (normal vs. patient: 350 ± 20 ng/mL vs. 2520 ± 37 ng/mL, p < 0.05). Furthermore, the proposed spectrofluorometric method exhibited improved linearity range, dynamic range, and detection limit compared to a previously reported spectrophotometric method.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Histamine, an essential compound synthesized from histidine through enzymatic decarboxylation, plays main role in regulating various physiological, immunological, and cognitive functions. It has a significant role in the pathogenesis of allergic rhinitis, a prevalent condition characterized by nasal inflammation and hypersensitivity reactions. Histamine rises in the nasal mucosa, particularly in the early response involving mast cell activation acute response, which is associated with mast cell activation, return towards baseline during a quiescent phase, and then rise again during the late phase [1–4]. The determination of histamine in nasal secretions can provide a specific indicator of local mast cell or basophil leukocyte activation in the nose. This makes it valuable for studying mast cell activation and diagnosing allergic reactions. However, there is controversy surrounding the levels of histamine in the nasal cavity in relation to allergenic stimulation [2,5]. This controversy suggests the presence of inaccuracies and imprecision in the analytical techniques, as well as the possible existence of quenching substances in nasal secretions. These factors can lead to an underestimation of histamine concentration, which should be taken into account and addressed in any developed analytical technique.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted significant global attention due to their diverse range of applications, including sensors, fluorescence bio imaging, drug delivery, and optical electronics. These carbon structures at the nanoscale level have emerged as promising optical probes for the identification of various compounds [6,7]. In comparison to conventional sensing probes based on organic dyes, CQDs possess unique fluorescence characteristics, eliminating concerns such as photo bleaching and toxicity [8]. Moreover, the surface of CQDs has functional groups, facilitating the selective functionalization process and offering remarkable sensing capabilities such as specificity and multiplex detection. Recently, there is a growing emphasis on utilizing natural and eco-friendly materials as carbon precursors for the non-toxic synthesis of CQDs.

The aim of this work is to develop a sensitive and selective spectrofluorometric method for assessing histamine levels in nasal mucus. The novelty lies in the synthesis and functionalization of CQDs derived from beetroot slices, as well as the introduction of histamine tetra phenyl borate for quenching the fluorescence of the CQDs. The characterization of the functionalized CQDs revealed their enhanced crystallinity, structural organization and unique fluorescence excitation-dependent emission behavior. The proposed method is based on the interaction between the functionalized CQDs and histamine resulted in concentration-dependent fluorescence quenching, enabling the determination of histamine levels in nasal secretions. The method was successfully applied to compare histamine levels in nasal secretions of healthy individuals and patients with allergic rhinitis. The results highlighted the association between allergic rhinitis and elevated histamine levels. Furthermore, the proposed method showed superior performance compared to a previously reported spectrophotometric method in terms of linearity range, dynamic range, and detection limit.

L-Histamine and sodium tetra phenyl borate was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, Germany. Dry fresh slices of beetroot (Beta vulgaris) was purchased from the local market. Several buffer solutions with different pH values were prepared in accordance with the US Pharmacopoeia.

Fluorescence measurements were performed using a Jasco FP −6200 Spectrofluorometer (Japan). UV–visible measurements were performed using a Shimadzu UV–Visible 1650 Spectrophotometer, (Japan. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were acquired using a JEOL JEM -M2100 transmission electron microscope at 200 Kv (USA). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was acquired using a PerkinElmer FTIR spectrophotometer (USA). X-ray diffractometry (XRD) was carried out using a Bruker AXS-D8 instrument (Germany) with CuKα radiation (λ = 1.5406 ˚A) in the 2θ range from 5◦ to 85◦ to analyze the prepared CQDs.

Stock standard solution of histamine (100 μg/mL) were made by dissolving 10 mg of pure reference histamine in 10 mL of 0.1 N hydrochloric acid and adjusting the volume to 100 mL with borate buffer solution, pH (8.0). Working solutions were made by suitable further dilutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of specific histamine tetra phenyl borate
ce:section.ce:para: The ion association complex, histamine tetra phenyl borate, was synthesized by combining 50 mL of 10−2 M solutions of histamine and sodium tetra phenyl borate. The resulting precipitate was allowed to remain in contact with the mother liquor for 6 h. Subsequently, the precipitate was filtered and thoroughly washed with distilled water. It was then left to dry at room temperature for 24 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQDs from beetroot slices
ce:section.ce:para: Dry fresh slices of beetroot (Beta vulgaris) was carbonized in an electric oven at 250° C for 50 min. The obtained product was allowed to cool before being ground into a fine powder. A weight equivalent to 400 mg of the product powder was dissolved in 60 mL.

double-distilled water, with additional washing step with dichloromethane to eliminate any remaining organic components. The resulting aqueous solution was subjected to centrifugation at 3000 rpm for 15 min, separating low-fluorescence precipitate. To further refine the product, excess acetone was added to the upper brown solution, followed by centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 15 min. This procedure yielded highly fluorescent beetroots- CQDs weighing approximately 200 mg.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functionalization of the prepared beetroots- CQDs with histamine tetra phenyl borate
ce:section.ce:para: A total of 100 mg of the synthesized beetroot- CQDs and 300 mg of histamine tetra phenyl borate were combined in a 100 mL volumetric flask. The mixture was dissolved in 50 mL of water and subjected to sonication for 2 h to achieve a uniform mixture. Subsequently, the mixture was filtered and dialyzed against deionized water for 12 h to eliminate any small unreacted molecules, byproducts, and larger particles. The resulting solution was then stored at a temperature of 4 °C for optimal usage as recommended.

Two different types of calibration curves were created in this study: a solvent calibration curve and a matrix-matched calibration curve. To create the solvent calibration curve, a series of histamine standard solutions ranging from 2 to 40 µg were mixed with 0.5 mL of the functionalized CQDs solution and 1 mL of borate buffer solution pH (8) in 10-mL volumetric flasks. The solutions were thoroughly allowed to stand at room temperature for 10 min. A blank sample was also prepared using the same conditions. After a 2-min interval, the fluorescence intensity of both the samples (F) and the blank sample (F0 ) was measured at 480 nm after excitation at 390 nm. The calibration curve was constructed by plotting the ratio of the blank sample fluorescence intensity to the sample fluorescence intensity (F0/F) against the concentration of histamine in ng/mL.

For the matrix-matched calibration curve, specific quantities of standard histamine solutions were added to tubes containing 0.1 mL of human nasal secretion. These standard solutions included quality control samples representing different concentrations: the lower limit of quantification (LLOQ) at 5 ng/mL, lower quantifiable concentration (LQC) at 25 ng/mL, middle quantifiable concentration (MQC) at 1500 ng/mL, high quantifiable concentration (HQC) at 3000 ng/mL, and the upper limit of quantification (ULOQ) at 3400 ng/mL. The samples were then treated with 3 mL of acetonitrile and centrifuged to eliminate cellular debris and protein content. The resulting supernatants were dried and reconstituted in 1 mL of borate buffer solution, pH (8) and mixed well. Subsequently, the prepared samples underwent the same procedures described earlier for the solvent calibration to construct the matrix-matched calibration curve. The matrix effect was evaluated using the following equation:
%
M a t r i x
e f f e c t =
Slopematrixcalibration
Slopesolventcalibration
. 100
The accuracy and precision of the developed method were assessed by evaluating the recovery and relative standard deviation (RSD), respectively. To determine the recovery and RSD, five replicates of histamine-spiked samples at lower quantifiable concentration (LQC), middle quantifiable concentration (MQC), and high quantifiable concentration (HQC) were analyzed. This assessment was performed on the same day to evaluate intra-day accuracy and precision, and on three different days to assess inter-day accuracy and precision. Additionally, to validate the applicability of the developed method in a clinical setting, spiked nasal secretion samples were analyzed using the established method. The results were expressed as recovery ± %RSD, providing insights on the specificity of the described method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of histamine levels in nasal secretions: Comparative evaluation of healthy volunteers and patients with allergic rhinitis
ce:section.ce:para: Two groups of subjects were recruited from the ENT Department of Damietta Faculty of Medicine, Al-Azhar University, Egypt. The first group consisted of 15 healthy participants with a median age of 35 years (range, 19 to 45 years). These individuals were in good general health, with no history of allergic rhinitis, other allergic conditions, or chronic medical conditions that could influence histamine levels. Additionally, they tested negative for nasal challenges to a panel of pollen extracts and negative for both skin prick tests and radio allegro sorbent tests. Participants who were currently using medications known to influence histamine levels were excluded from this group. The second group comprised 21 allergic rhinitis patients with a median age of 38 years (range, 18 to 48 years). These patients had a well-documented history of allergic rhinitis and demonstrated positive results in both the skin prick test and radio allegro sorbent test. Furthermore, their allergic rhinitis was confirmed through positive nasal-challenge tests based on symptom scores. Patients with nasal or systemic diseases that could influence histamine levels, as well as those currently using medications that could affect histamine levels, were excluded from this group. Informed consent was obtained from each individual, and the study was approved by the Ethics committee at Damietta Faculty of Medicine, Al-Azhar University, Egypt (DFM-IRB 00012367-23-01-003).

Nasal secretion samples were collected by instilling 3 mL of sterile borate buffer solution, pH (8) into each participant's naris, and the expelled secretions were collected in a plastic petri dish. To consider the unknown concentrations of histamine in the diluted nasal secretions, the standard addition technique was employed. Specific volumes of a standard histamine solution were added to 5 mL aliquots of the diluted nasal secretions. The fluorescence intensity was measured and used to calculate the histamine concentration using the following equation:
C x
=
C S
V s
V x
+
V s
10
n
Δ F
s
-
V x
V s
+
V x
Where
C x
and
V x
represent the unknown concentration and the volume, respectively,
C S
and
V s
represent the known added histamine solution concentration and its added volume, respectively, S is the slope of the respective calibration curve and
Δ F
is the measured fluorescence intensity change.

The determination of histamine in nasal secretions serves as a specific indicator of mast cell or basophil leukocyte activation, making it valuable for studying mast cell activation and diagnosing allergic reactions. However, the controversy surrounding histamine levels in nasal secretions in relation to allergenic stimulation indicates potential inaccuracies and imprecision in the analytical methods used, as well as the presence of substances that may interfere with the detection of histamine. These factors can result in imprecise estimation of histamine concentration, highlighting the need for an improved analytical method [2,5]. Therefore, the objective of this study is to develop a modified spectrofluorometric method using CQDs derived from beetroot slices and functionalized with histamine tetra phenyl borate to determine histamine levels in nasal mucus. The quenching ability of the synthesized functionalized fluorescent CQDs was utilized for histamine determination. Furthermore, the method successfully determined histamine concentrations in nasal secretions and identified elevated levels in patients with allergic rhinitis. Notably, this method exhibited superior performance compared to a previously reported spectrophotometric method in terms of linearity, dynamic range, and detection limit.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of functionalized CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Preparation of functionalized CQDs with histamine tetra phenyl borate, modifying the surface of CQDs and improving their selectively to detect histamine. This process was provided through 2 steps: The first step was preparing CQDs from beetroot slices, that rich in carbohydrates, particularly in the form of sugars, and the primary sugar found is sucrose [9,10]. Under carbonization using microwave assisted method, the high temperature causes the breakdown of sucrose present in the beetroot slices. This thermal decomposition leads dehydration of sucrose and generating soluble products such as furfural compounds, organic acids, aldehydes, and phenols. Furthermore, polymerization and condensation of these products lead to soluble polymers. Aromatic clusters form through aldol condensation, cycloaddition, and hydroxyl methyl-mediated furan resin condensation. CQDs form when the concentration of aromatic clusters reaches a critical super-saturation point, resulting in a burst nucleation event [11]. The second step was: the functionalizing of CQDs with histamine tetra phenyl borate through covalent bonding. Histamine tetra phenyl borate was prepared based on the fact that, histamine behaves as a cation, due to the presence of amino group. This suggested the use of anionic type of ion exchanger such as tetra phenyl borate as counter ions to prepare water insoluble association complex using precipitation based technique. The resulting product can provide reactive functional groups, the carboxylic acid groups. on the surface of CQDs.

X-ray diffraction (XRD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy techniques were used together to characterize the synthesized CQDs. The XRD analysis, demonstrate the crystal structure and lattice spacing of the material, provided diffraction patterns for both non-functionalized and functionalized CQDs, Fig. 1 a. Specifically, the peaks observed at 2θ = 22.3 and 2θ = 23.1 for non-functionalized CQDs and functionalized CQDs respectively, was attributed to the (002) lattice spacing of carbon-based materials, indicating the graphitic nature of carbon [12]. However, the XRD patterns of the functionalized CQDs exhibited narrower and sharper peaks compared to the non-functionalized CQDs. This was attributed to the introduction of additional functional groups, namely (CO) and (NH), into the structure of the functionalized CQDs [12,13]. These functional groups alter the arrangement and interactions within the CQDs, resulting in a more ordered and compact structure. The narrower and sharper peaks observed in the XRD analysis indicated formation of amorphous nanoparticles with higher degree of crystallinity and improved structural organization in the functionalized CQDs [14].

The morphology and core size of the prepared non-functionalized and functionalized beetroots-CQDs were examined using TEM. Both CQDs exhibited a spherical morphology, as shown in Fig. 1b and c. The average core sizes of the CQDs were determined using particle size distribution plots (inset, Fig. 1b and c), which indicated that the non-functionalized CQDs had an average core size of 2.0 ± 0.22 nm, while the functionalized CQDs had an average core size of 1.5 ± 0.18 nm. Additionally, the functionalized CQDs displayed a uniform distribution of spherical shapes and a more condensed structure compared to the non-functionalized CQDs. This can be attributed to the presence of carboxyl and amino groups on the surface of the CQDs resulting from the functionalization process. Furthermore, the functionalization process resulted in a clear surface for the CQDs. This was achieved through the hybridization of COH groups from the carbon constituents and the formation of covalent bonds between the reactive functional groups on the surface of the CQDs and the amine groups present in the histamine ion association complex.

FTIR analysis was utilized to complement the XRD and TEM results in order to identify the functional groups present on the surface of the non-functionalized and functionalized beetroots-CQDs, Fig. 2 . Both types of CQDs exhibited a prominent peak at 3350–3600 cm−1, indicating presence of hydroxyl groups on their surfaces and highlighting surface activation. The distinct peaks observed at 1700 cm−1 –1800 cm−1, suggested the existence of carbonyl groups, indicating the presence of carboxylic acid groups. However, the FTIR spectrum of the functionalized CQDs showed a strong, broad absorption band at 3440 cm−1, indicating the presence of an amine group. Additionally, a series of medium and strong absorption bands in the range of 1500–1600 cm−1 were attributed to the C stretching vibrations of the aromatic ring present in the functionalized beetroot-CQDs [15–17].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of the proposed functionalized CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical characteristics of the functionalized CQDs were examined using UV–visible and fluorescence spectroscopy techniques. In the UV–visible absorption spectrum of the functionalized CQDs, a characteristic absorption peak at 281 nm was observed, which extended slightly into the visible range. This absorption feature is attributed to the electronic transitions of the CC and CO bands, namely the π–π* and n–π* transitions, respectively, Fig. 3 a [17]. Moreover, upon excitation at 390 nm, the functionalized CQDs exhibited a strong fluorescence emission peak at 480 nm, Fig. 3b, which arises from the recombination of excited electrons and holes within the structure of the CQDs. The emission wavelength and intensity are greatly influenced by the excitation wavelength and energy, so a study was conducted to investigate the dependence of the emission on the excitation wavelength, which was systematically varied from 390 to 450 nm. This investigation revealed unique characteristics of the functionalized CQDs. Additionally, as the excitation wavelength was increased, the fluorescence emission peak exhibited a red shift, moving from 480 to 515 nm. This shift in emission wavelength was attributed to changes in the energy levels of the excited states within the CQDs, resulting from the altered excitation conditions. Furthermore, there was a significant decrease in fluorescence intensity as the excitation wavelength increased, indicating the excitation-dependent emission behavior of the functionalized CQDs, Fig. 3c [18,19].

The quantum yield of the functionalized CQDs was calculated using a comparative single-point method using quinine sulfate as a reference standard in 0.01 M H2SO4 at an excitation wavelength of 390 nm according to the following equation [20,21]
φ
s =
φ r
A r
A s
E s
E r
η
s
2
η
r
2
Where, Φ is fluorescence quantum yield, η is a refractive index of the solvent, A is absorbance of the solution, E is integrated fluorescence intensity area of the emitted light, and subscript “r” and “s” refer to the reference and sample, respectively. The quantum yield of the functionalized CQDs was found to be 28.28 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed interaction and quenching of the functionalized CQDs by histamine; quantitative analysis impact
ce:section.ce:para: The dynamic quenching mechanism of fluorescence in beetroots-functionalized CQDs relies on the collisional interaction between the excited state of the CQDs and histamine molecules. This interaction was facilitated through energy transfer, involving the transfer of an electron from the excited state of the CQDs to histamine, resulting in the deactivation of the CQDs excited state. To achieve this, the surface of the CQDs was improved through functionalization and activation of their carboxylic acid groups. This modification allows the CQDs to react with the amine groups of histamine, which is a selective recognizing compound known as histamine tetra phenyl borate. Consequently, the functionalized surface of the CQDs interacts with histamine, leading to a decrease in the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs. The concentration of histamine plays a vital role in the quenching process. As the histamine concentration increases, a greater number of histamine molecules come into contact with the functionalized CQDs, resulting in a more pronounced decrease in fluorescence intensity. This concentration-dependent fluorescence quenching can be quantitatively measured, as depicted in Fig. 4 , where the fluorescence intensity of the functionalized CQDs gradually decreases as the histamine concentration increases, providing a visual representation of this phenomenon.

Investigation of the factors that influence the fluorescence quenching of beetroots-functionalized CQDs by histamine is essential to optimize and maximize the quenching process. Several parameters were examined, including the pH and volume of the buffer, the volume of beetroots-functionalized CQDs, and the incubation time. To assess the effect of pH on the quenching process, analysis was conducted with and without the addition of buffer solutions at various pH levels ranging from 3 to 10. The results revealed that a borate buffer solution, pH (8) achieved uniform quenching, as shown in Fig. 5 a. To determine the optimal volume of the buffer solution, different volumes were tested, and the results demonstrated that 1 mL provided the best results, as shown in Fig. 5b. Furthermore, the influence of varying volumes of the beetroots-functionalized CQDs solution on the fluorescence quenching was examined. The findings revealed that 0.5 mL of the CQDs solution yielded the best fluorescence quenching when combined with histamine, as shown in Fig. 5c. Additionally, the kinetics of the fluorescence quenching over different time intervals, ranging from 0 to 30 min, were investigated. The results showed that maximum fluorescence quenching of CQDs was achieved within 2 min, indicating a rapid interaction between the functionalized CQDs and histamine. No further quenching was observed even with prolonged reaction times as shown in Fig. 5d.

The method described was evaluated following the ICH M10 guidelines, which include assessing linearity, accuracy, precision, and matrix effect [22]. A summary of the obtained results was presented in Table 1 . The evaluation showed that the method displayed excellent linearity across a concentration range of 25–3000 ng/mL, with a coefficient of determination (r2) of 0.9991, indicating excellent linear relationship. Moreover, the method exhibited high sensitivity, as evidenced by a lower limit of quantification (LLOQ) of 5 ng/mL. To assess the matrix effect, calibration curves were constructed using both solvent and matrix-matched samples, and the ratio of their slopes was calculated. A ratio of 100.09 % indicated that the matrix had no significant impact on the precision of the method. Accuracy and precision were evaluated by analyzing LQC, MQC, and HQC samples in replicates of five. These analyses were performed on the same day and repeated on three different days. The results, as shown in Table 1, demonstrated that the method adhered to the accepted values for accuracy and precision.

In order to assess the robustness of the method, slight variations were introduced to parameters that could potentially influence the results. Minor changes were made to the buffer pH (8 ± 0.5), buffer volume (1 ± 0.25 mL), CQD volume (0.5 ± 0.1 mL), and incubation time (2 ± 1 min). The impact of these parameter adjustments on the method performance was assessed and the results are presented in Table 1. These minor variations did not have a significant effect on the method reliability and accuracy.

Furthermore, the method specificity was tested analyzing different concentration of histamine in spiked nasal secretion samples. This was done validating the applicability of the method in clinical settings. The results, outlined in Table 2 , affirmed the specificity of the applied procedures and the dependability of the method for clinical applications. In addition, the nasal matrix had no impact on the measurements, highlighting the robustness and suitability of the method for analyzing clinical samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectrofluorometric determination of histamine in the nasal secretions: Comparison between healthy volunteers and patients with allergic rhinitis
ce:section.ce:para: The developed spectrofluorometric method was utilized to determine the concentration of histamine in the nasal secretions of healthy volunteers and patients with allergic rhinitis. The standard addition technique was employed by adding a known volume of standard histamine solution to aliquots of diluted nasal samples with unknown histamine concentrations. The histamine concentration of the samples was determined as proposed. The analysis of nasal secretion samples confirmed the presence of histamine in both the healthy group and the patients with allergic rhinitis. Notably, the data presented in Table 3 revealed that the levels of histamine were found to be significantly higher in patients with allergic rhinitis compared to the healthy group (normal vs. patient: 350 ± 20 ng/mL vs. 2520 ± 37 ng/mL, p < 0.05). These findings indicate a clear association between allergic rhinitis and elevated histamine levels in nasal secretions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparative evaluation of the proposed method and previously reported spectrophotometric for histamine determination
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed spectrofluorometric method offers advantages in terms of linearity range, dynamic range, detection limit making it a potentially favorable choice for histamine determination compared to the previously reported spectrophotometric method [23], as summarized in Table 4 . The proposed method demonstrates a broader range of linearity, 25–3000 ng/mL, compared to the narrower range of 200–1000 ng/mL in the reported method. It also offers a wide dynamic range, allowing determination of histamine across a broad spectrum, whereas the reported method has a limited dynamic range. In addition, the described method exhibits a lower detection limit of 5 ng/mL, indicating higher sensitivity compared to the reported method detection limit of 200 ng/mL.

A sensitive and selective spectrofluorometric method was developed using carbon quantum dots (CQDs) derived from beetroot slices for sensitive and selective determination of histamine in nasal secretions. These CQDs were functionalized with histamine tetra phenyl borate to determine histamine levels in nasal secretions. The functionalized CQDs exhibited enhanced fluorescence and showed concentration-dependent fluorescence quenching in the presence of histamine. The method was successfully applied to compare histamine levels in healthy individuals and patients with allergic rhinitis, revealing elevated histamine levels in the latter group. The proposed method offers improved linearity range, dynamic range, and detection limit compared to previous methods.

Saleh l. Alaqel: Methodology. Majed A Algarni: Validation. Adnan Alharbi: Validation. Atiah H. Almalki: Methodology. Mohammad S. Alzahrani: Methodology. Mohamed H. Abdelazim: . Ahmed H. Abdelazim: Conceptualization, Project administration, Methodology, Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The researchers would like to acknowledge Deanship of Scientific Research, Taif University for funding this work.
